attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old man in an urban setting presents to his general practitioner with a two-day history of low back pain. The pain came on suddenly when he was lifting a heavy piece of furniture and has now started to radiate down his left leg. He has never had pain of this severity before and has had to stop his work as a bus driver. His medical history includes mild anxiety. He is not currently taking any medications. On examination, he has a high steppage gait, is tender over the L4/5 region, and has a reduced range of motion with lumbar flexion, extension, and rotation because of pain. He also exhibits loss of power in dorsiflexion of the left foot. His knee jerk and ankle jerk reflexes are present, but the ankle jerk is difficult to elicit. He has a normal plantar reflex. Routine bloods as part of his annual wellness check one month ago showed a normal renal function. It was noted that the patient has a history of anaphylactic reactions to ketorolac.

A same day MRI was conducted, and showed a disc prolapse.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most important immediate step?
attributes.explanation:
This man presents with acute lower back pain (<6 weeks) with clinical features suggestive of a prolapsed intervertebral disc. He presents with lower back pain localised to the L4/L5 region and lumbar radicular pain which suggests nerve root involvement and there may be signs of progressive neurological deficits as there is difficulty in eliciting the right ankle jerk reflex. As well, he has high steppage gait. It is also noted that this patient's MRI findings confirm the occurrence of a prolapsed disc.

There are a number of factors (such as examination findings, and extent of symptoms) that need to be considered in the management of a prolapsed intervertebral disc, and there are differing recommendations depending on the different presentations. While there are some disagreements regarding some forms of management, and when they should be commenced, there are some areas where there is agreement.

Immediate specialist referral - there is a consistent recommendation across guidelines for specialist referral for lumbar disc herniation under two circumstances: 1) after no improvement of symptoms with conservative management 2) immediately if the patient shows a high steppage gait. 3) Imaging findings of disc prolapse. As this patient has a high steppage gait, and in consideration of the imaging findings, immediate specialist referral is the correct answer here. The loss of power in dorsiflexion of the left foot indicates potential nerve root compression, which requires urgent evaluation to prevent permanent neurological damage.

Manage with ibuprofen - the goal of pharmacological management is to reduce rather than eliminate pain, so that physical function can be maintained. First-line treatment typically involves a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID), such as ibuprofen, for short-term pain relief. This is supported by a systematic review and meta-analysis which concluded that NSAIDs are effective for short-term symptomatic relief in patients with acute lower back pain with or without sciatica. However, as the patient is showing a high steppage gait, this answer is incorrect. Furthermore, of even more relevance is that the patient has a past history of anaphylaxis after exposure to ketorolac (an NSAID); considering this dangerous reaction to NSAIDs, it is urged that these be avoided in the patient.

Manage with oxycodone slow release - Opioids may be considered if patients have severe pain that is not adequately relieved with other measures or significantly impairs their ability to function. In this scenario, opioids should only be prescribed on a short-term trial basis, as part of an overall pain management strategy. However, this would not be the first appropriate step for this patient. Furthermore, we would not be likely to be providing a slow-release opioid for short-term usage.

Epidural injection - There are inconsistent recommendations in guidelines for the usage of epidural injections in lumbar disc herniation. However, this would be far less of a priority compared to specialist management.

Commence physiotherapy - While conservative management with physiotherapy is an important part of management, it would not be the most appropriate next step in this scenario.
learningPoints: Patients with lumbar disc herniation (and imaging findings demonstrating disc prolapse) who show high steppage gait should be immediately referred for specialist management.
Pain management with NSAIDs have a role in the treatment acute lower back pain.
references: Gregory DS, Seto CK, Wortley GC, et al. Acute Lumbar Disk Pain: Navigating Evaluation and Treatment Choices Am Fam Phys. 2008; 7:835-42

Machado GC, Maher CG, Ferreira PH, Day RO, Pinheiro MB, Ferreira ML. Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs for spinal pain: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Annals of the Rheumatic Diseases. 2017;76(7):1269-78.

Machado GC, Maher CG, Ferreira PH, Pinheiro MB, Lin C-WC, Day RO, et al. Efficacy and safety of paracetamol for spinal pain and osteoarthritis: systematic review and meta-analysis of randomised placebo controlled trials. BMJ : British Medical Journal. 2015;350:h1225.

Khorami AK, Oliveira CB, Maher CG, Bindels PJE, Machado GC, Pinto RZ, Koes BW, Chiarotto A. Recommendations for Diagnosis and Treatment of Lumbosacral Radicular Pain: A Systematic Review of Clinical Practice Guidelines. Journal of Clinical Medicine. 2021; 10(11):2482. https://doi.org/10.3390/jcm10112482
